OIIX A3714/25.explanation: This is an important message for foreign airlines and aircraft flying to, from, or through Iran. It states that they must now send their flight plans and any updates to a specific address (OIIXIFPS). Also, all international flights entering Iran's airspace or landing at Iranian airports must follow new procedures detailed in Iran's aviation rulebook.

OIIX A3715/25.explanation: This is an aviation alert for the Tehran airspace. Several specific high-frequency radio channels (5658 KHZ, 5667 KHZ, 6925 KHZ, 8091 KHZ, 8918 KHZ, 10018 KHZ, 13288 KHZ, and 13312 KHZ) used for communication with Tehran Information are currently not working. This affects all aircraft flying in the Tehran Flight Information Region. The issue started on December 11, 2025, and is expected to continue until March 11, 2026.

OIIX A3716/25.explanation: A new controlled airspace has been established south of Tehran. This means that from about 19,500 feet upwards, aircraft flying in this specific area will be under the control of Tehran Radar. Pilots will need to communicate on frequency 133.400 MHz in English or Persian. This change is permanent and helps manage air traffic in the region.

OIIX A3717/25.explanation: This notice informs pilots that several radio frequencies used for air traffic control communication in the Tehran region (OIIX) are changing. Pilots flying in this area will need to use the new frequency, 258.400 MHz, for communication in various sectors. This change is active from December 11, 2025, to March 11, 2026.

OIIX A3718/25.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots flying in Iran. It states that several specific air routes (like R659, G666, etc.) can now be flown using a more modern and efficient navigation method called RNAV5. This means aircraft with the right equipment can use their onboard systems to fly these routes more directly, starting from the lowest safe altitude for each route. This change is active from December 11, 2025, until March 11, 2026.

OIIX A3810/25.explanation: A radar system in the Tehran region of Iran is broken and will be out of service from December 2025 to March 2026. This means air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in certain parts of the sky, specifically in some areas where planes fly between cities. Pilots flying through these areas need to be aware that radar services might not be available, which could affect how air traffic is managed.

OIIX B0995/25.explanation: This is an update about specific, required flight paths for domestic planes flying between two airports (OIII and OIMD) within Iran's airspace. Planes must now follow these exact routes.

OIIX A0198/26.explanation: This is an update to the flight rules and procedures within Iran's airspace (Tehran FIR). It specifically changes the routes and takeoff/landing paths (called SIDs and STARs) for several domestic flights between certain airports, like from Kerman to Mashhad, and from Shiraz to Bandar Abbas, Kish Island, and Chah Bahar. These changes are part of a larger plan to manage air traffic and are detailed in an aviation manual.

OIIX B0062/26.explanation: Starting January 25, 2026, and lasting for a few months, all private planes and planes flying by visual rules (VFR) are not allowed to fly in Iran. The only exceptions are flights for hospitals, search and rescue, government, military, and specific oil company helicopters, but even these need special permission beforehand. Flights operating within airport traffic zones are also exempt.

OIIX A0671/26.explanation: A crucial radio beacon called ANK VOR, which helps aircraft navigate, is currently broken and cannot be used. It will be out of service from February 23, 2026, until at least May 24, 2026. Pilots flying in the area will need to use alternative navigation methods. Once it's repaired, it will undergo a flight test to ensure it's working correctly.

OIIX A0712/26.explanation: An important air route, called N39, in Tehran's airspace (between DEMBA and OBRIX) will be closed for all planes flying through that area. This closure will last from February 28 to May 29, 2026. Planes flying west will need to use an alternative route through RADAL, IMKER, and ULDUS, flying no lower than 27,500 feet. Planes flying east will need to use an alternative route through ULDUS, ALKUP, IMLIM, OXADU, and OBRIX, flying no lower than 28,500 feet.

OIIX A0713/26.explanation: An air highway (called G208/L125) in the airspace over Tehran, Iran, is temporarily closed for planes that are just passing through the area. If a pilot planned to use this specific route, they now need to fly a different, specified path (via ROVAD direct to IKA). However, planes arriving at Tehran's main airports (Imam Khomeini, Mehrabad, and Payam) can still use a specific entry point (RADAL). This change is in effect from February 28 to May 10, 2026.

OIIX A0724/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran region. It provides new, additional phone numbers (a direct line and a mobile number) to contact Air Traffic Control.

OIIX B0185/26.explanation: This is a routine aviation message, like a 'table of contents' for pilots and air traffic controllers in the Tehran region. It lists all the important, currently active aviation notices (NOTAMs), updates to official aviation publications (AIP Amendments and Supplements), and informational circulars (AICs). It also mentions that a specific update won't be published on March 19, 2026, and refers to a particular type of NOTAM. Essentially, it helps ensure everyone has the most up-to-date information for safe flying.

OIIX A0743/26.explanation: This is a routine aviation message for pilots and air traffic controllers in the Tehran, Iran region. It doesn't announce a new problem or change, but rather provides a comprehensive list of all currently active aviation warnings (NOTAMs) from 2022 to 2026. It also lists the latest updates to official aviation documents like the Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP), its supplements, and circulars. Additionally, it informs that a specific update (AIRAC AIP AMDT) will not be published on March 19, 2026. Think of it as a master list to help ensure everyone has the most current information for safe flying.

OIIX A0750/26.explanation: This is a warning for all flights in the Persian Gulf and Oman Sea, within the airspace managed by Tehran. It says that due to security reasons, an area where there might be conflict could expand into international waters. Pilots should be very careful when flying in this region between April 14 and April 18, 2026.

OIIX A0751/26.explanation: The airspace over Tehran (Iran) is currently operating under special conditions. The eastern part of the airspace is now open for flights, but the western part remains closed. Flights that need to pass through the open section must follow strict rules: they need to contact air traffic control at specific times, ensure their aircraft have working collision avoidance systems, and might be kept further apart from other planes than usual. These restrictions are in place until April 25, 2026.

OIIX A0752/26.explanation: This is an urgent aviation notice for Iran. Starting April 18, 2026, at 08:30 AM UTC until April 25, 2026, at 02:30 PM UTC, most airports in Iran are closed. Only six specific airports (OIII, OIIE, OING, OIMM, OIMB, and OIZH) will be operational, and only during limited hours (from 03:30 AM to 02:30 PM UTC). All previously granted flight permissions are cancelled. Airlines wishing to operate civil passenger flights to these open airports must request new permission from the Iran Civil Aviation Authority.

KICZ A0016/20.explanation: The U.S. government is warning American airlines, pilots, and aircraft operators to be very careful when flying over the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. This is due to ongoing military activity and political tensions in the region. They should review security information, avoid routes close to Iranian airspace to prevent being mistaken for something else by air defense systems, and be prepared for potential GPS and communication jamming. They must also report any safety or security incidents. A separate, stricter warning that bans U.S. flights over Iranian airspace is still in effect.

KICZ A0011/24.explanation: The United States government has issued a new rule (SFAR 117) that bans certain US flights from flying in the airspace over Tehran, Iran. This ban started on October 3, 2024, and is expected to last until October 31, 2027. All pilots and airlines are required to read the full details of this rule.

OIIX A0761/26.explanation: This is an urgent notice for airplanes flying over Iran (Tehran airspace). It states that all previous rules for planes flying through this airspace are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes must now be followed for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially during a 'recovery period' (suggesting things are getting back to normal after an incident). It provides detailed routes for planes flying between Muscat (Oman) and Bahrain, with a slightly different route for planes heading to the UAE.

OIIX A0753/26.explanation: A specific danger zone, OID126, in the Tehran region will be active daily from April 20 to May 10, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 04:30 PM UTC. Aircraft should avoid flying in this area from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level during these times as it is considered dangerous.
